med_suggestions.exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible and near term; consider drug interactions; adjust/avoid in significant renal impairment per clinician judgment.

note.soap:
S:  
54-year-old adult presents with a red and swollen leg. There is a wound serving as a portal of entry. Signs of limb infection are all present, including purulence. The redness is rapidly spreading. There are no systemic symptoms reported. The patient is not immunocompromised and has no known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant.

O:  
Phone history only; no physical exam or vital signs available. Patient reports a wound on the leg with purulent drainage and rapidly spreading redness and swelling. No systemic symptoms such as fever or malaise reported.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely):  
  Cellulitis with purulent wound infection and rapidly spreading erythema.  

- Differential:  
  1. Cellulitis with abscess formation  
  2. Necrotizing soft tissue infection (less likely without pain out of proportion or systemic symptoms)  
  3. Deep vein thrombosis (less likely given purulence and wound)  
  4. Contact dermatitis with secondary infection  

- Red flags considered:  
  Rapidly spreading infection, purulence, presence of wound as portal of entry, no systemic symptoms or immunocompromise.

P:  
- Disposition:  
  Urgent in-person evaluation recommended for physical examination, possible incision and drainage, and assessment of severity. Consider emergency care if systemic symptoms develop or rapid progression continues.  

- Plan: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) — 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 5–7 days. Alternative: Clindamycin (300–450 mg PO TID x 5–7 days).
  Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
  - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
  - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
  - Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) — 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 5–7 days
    - Notes: Preferred when purulence/abscess features or MRSA risk is present. Not ideal monotherapy for classic nonpurulent cellulitis (strep coverage may be needed).
    - Exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible and near term; consider drug interactions; adjust/avoid in significant renal impairment per clinician judgment.
  - Clindamycin — 300–450 mg PO TID x 5–7 days
    - Notes: Alternative if beta-lactam allergy or when MRSA coverage is needed (local resistance patterns apply).
    - Exclusions: History of C. difficile/high CDI risk; lincosamide allergy.
  - Cephalexin — 500 mg PO QID x 5–7 days
    - Notes: First-line for uncomplicated NONPURULENT cellulitis when MRSA risk is low.
    - Exclusions: Immediate-type severe beta-lactam allergy; adjust in renal impairment per clinician judgment.
  - Amoxicillin-clavulanate — 875/125 mg PO BID x 5–7 days
    - Notes: Option when bite/wound flora or polymicrobial concern (clinician judgment).
    - Exclusions: Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; adjust in renal impairment.
  - Supportive care:
  - Local care: Elevation of affected area, gentle cleansing, avoid picking; mark border if feasible to track spread.
  - Pain/fever: Acetaminophen if safe; avoid NSAIDs in late pregnancy, renal disease, ulcer/GI bleed risk.
  - Follow-up and safety:
  - Expected course: Some redness may persist early; should not rapidly expand after 24–48h of therapy.
  - Re-evaluation: In-person evaluation if worsening after 24–48h, rapidly spreading erythema, severe pain, systemic symptoms, or inability to take PO.
  - Urgent/ED now: Fever with toxicity, rapidly progressive infection, severe pain out of proportion, immunocompromise, facial involvement/eye symptoms, necrosis, or concern for sepsis.

- Safety net:  
  Advise patient to seek immediate care if fever, chills, increased pain, spreading redness, swelling, or systemic symptoms develop. Return for reassessment if no improvement within 48 hours or worsening occurs.
patient_instructions: This appears to be a skin infection (cellulitis). Some redness and swelling can persist early, but symptoms should start to stabilize or improve within 24–48 hours once treatment begins.
Go to urgent care the same day if redness is rapidly spreading, pain is worsening, you develop fever or chills, or the area becomes more swollen or tender.
Go to the emergency department immediately if you feel very ill, develop confusion, have severe pain out of proportion, rapidly progressing redness, or signs of sepsis.
Because this is a phone visit, we cannot examine the area. If you cannot get in-person care promptly or symptoms are not improving within 48 hours, please get evaluated in person.
